file_verdicts.summary: Standard AppImage packaging with no malicious behavior.

Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD contains only variable assignments (pkgname, pkgver, etc.) and a single `source` array entry pointing to an HTTPS URL on the official Beeper download domain. No commands are executed at global scope (no command substitutions, no function calls). There is no `pkgver()` function defined, so no code runs during `makepkg --printsrcinfo` or the sourcing phase. The build and package functions (which contain AppImage extraction, sed, and asar operations) are not executed during the narrow gate because of the `--nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` flags. Therefore, fetching the sources and sourcing the PKGBUILD is safe.
</details>
<summary>No code execution in global scope or pkgver(). Safe to fetch.</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- No code execution in global scope or pkgver(). Safe to fetch.[/]

! Reviewed Beeper-4.2.942-x86_64.AppImage. Status: SKIPPED -- Skipping binary file: Beeper-4.2.942-x86_64.AppImage
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD downloads the official AppImage from `beeper-desktop.download.beeper.com` with a pinned SHA-256 checksum. It extracts the AppImage and patches two files:  
1. The `AppRun` script to set `APPDIR` to the install path (standard for relocating AppImages).  
2. A JavaScript file exporting `registerLinuxConfig` to replace it with a no-op function (likely to suppress unwanted behavior in the packaged Electron app).  

No obfuscated code, network requests to unknown hosts, base64 decoding, `eval`, or data exfiltration are present. All operations are confined to the package build directory and the expected install paths (`/opt/beeper`, `/usr/bin`). The patching of `registerLinuxConfig` is a routine modification for binary redistribution and does not exfiltrate data or execute attacker-controlled code.
</details>
<summary>Standard AppImage packaging with no malicious behavior.</summary>
</security_assessment>

file_verdicts.summary: Legitimate binary packaging with safe, targeted patching

audits.body:
Cloning https://aur.archlinux.org/beeper-bin.git to /tmp/aur-sleuth/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-bek5fdlu...
Cloned repository to /tmp/aur-sleuth/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-bek5fdlu
Analyzing beeper-bin AUR package (working in /tmp/aur-sleuth/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-bek5fdlu) with qwen/qwen3-235b-a22b-2507 from https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines a single source pointing to an AppImage from the official beeper.com domain, which is consistent with the package's upstream. The `sha256sums` array contains a valid checksum, and there are no uses of command substitution, `eval`, or dynamic fetching in the global scope or `pkgver()` function that would execute during `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare`. The `pkgver` is hardcoded, so no external calls are needed to determine it. Sourcing the PKGBUILD and downloading the source during this phase involves only standard, safe operations: fetching a known AppImage over HTTPS and verifying its checksum. No destructive, network-exfiltrating, or code-executing actions occur at this stage.

While the `build()` and `package()` functions contain logic that modifies the AppImage contents (such as patching an `.asar` file and disabling a `registerLinuxConfig` export), these functions are not executed due to the `--nobuild` and `--noprepare` flags. Therefore, they are out of scope for this narrow safety gate. The source retrieval and PKGBUILD parsing are free of malicious behavior under the current constraints.
</details>
<summary>
No dangerous operations during source fetch and parse
</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- No dangerous operations during source fetch and parse[/]

! Reviewed Beeper-4.2.942-x86_64.AppImage. Status: SKIPPED -- Skipping binary file: Beeper-4.2.942-x86_64.AppImage
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD retrieves a single AppImage from Beeper's official download domain (`beeper-desktop.download.beeper.com`) and validates it with a fixed sha256sum, which is standard and secure practice for binary packages. The build process extracts the AppImage and modifies the `AppRun` script to respect the installation path, which is a common customization in AUR packages.

In the `_package_beeper` function, the package uses `asar extract` to unpack the Electron app bundle, which is expected for applications built with Electron. It searches for a specific export pattern in `.mjs` files — `export{[a-zA-Z0-9_]+ as registerLinuxConfig};` — and patches it to noop the `registerLinuxConfig` function using `sed`. This patching is limited in scope and appears to disable a Linux-specific configuration routine, likely to prevent conflicts or errors on Arch Linux. This kind of runtime patching, while somewhat invasive, is not uncommon when downstream packagers must adapt Electron apps to distribution standards.

No network requests occur during build or package phases beyond the initial source download. There is no obfuscated code, no execution of external scripts, and no data exfiltration. The modifications are transparent, targeted, and scoped to enable proper integration. The package also installs standard desktop metadata and licenses. All operations are consistent with legitimate packaging of a closed-source Electron app for Arch Linux.
</details>
<summary>
Legitimate binary packaging with safe, targeted patching</summary>
</security_assessment>

Audit Skips:

/tmp/aur-sleuth/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-bek5fdlu/Beeper-4.2.942-x86_64.AppImage: [SKIPPED] Skipping binary file: Beeper-4.2.942-x86_64.AppImage

data.reasoning: Both models reviewed the PKGBUILD, which is the only text/script file in this package and the sole thing that executes during makepkg. The PKGBUILD downloads a single AppImage from Beeper's official domain (beeper-desktop.download.beeper.com) with a pinned SHA-256 checksum, extracts it, patches the AppRun script for path relocation, and noops a registerLinuxConfig export via sed. No network calls beyond the verified source, no eval/obfuscation, no install hooks, and all filesystem operations are scoped to standard install paths (/opt/beeper, /usr/bin). The binary AppImage cannot be statically reviewed and was appropriately skipped — its contents are upstream-controlled and checksum-pinned, which is the standard trust model for -bin packages.
learnings: The 'shallow' trigger (1 file reviewed) is misleading here: the package only contains a PKGBUILD and a binary AppImage. Reviewing 1 of 2 files where the other is an unreviewable binary is actually complete coverage. The trigger heuristic should account for whether skipped files are binaries with no static analysis value before flagging shallowness.

Both models correctly identified that build()/package() functions are out of scope for the narrow makepkg gate but still reviewed their content in the second pass — good behavior that should be reinforced.

For -bin packages, the binary blob is inherently uninspectable; the meaningful trust anchor is the checksum + source domain. The tool could explicitly note this trust model rather than treating the skipped binary as a coverage gap.

The sed patch noop'ing registerLinuxConfig is a slightly unusual modification; future audits could benefit from a flag noting 'upstream behavior modified' even when benign, to aid human reviewers without raising it to unsafe.
